and we got the whole Sandstone team too.

things?

if it, if it's a new build?

it, it has a website, it just won't mention online payments.
I mean,
so I feel like that's the assumption most places have it, if not all.
So, I guess two questions off of that.
So if it's not mentioned on the website, what does it default to?
And if there is conflicting information on, let's say the BH website and then apartments.com, which one does it pull in?
Yeah.
So it's, if it's not on the website, we, we, we would never assume the presence of an amenity just because it has a website.

So it's going to be, if, if it's not listed directly on the website, then it's getting pulled in from another public source, most likely, uh, a large, uh I OS, um, in terms of how we weigh those two, we always look at a website and privilege a website first.
Uh, and then we'll go to, uh, uh, an internet listing service after that.
Are you seeing the results that are different than what you'd expect there?
Yeah.

So some, some properties are saying that they have it and some don't, but really they all do,
we can absolutely set a default right now it's, it, um, it's ne it just needs to be listed online for us to pick it up.
So we're not making any assumptions across like portfolios or anything like that.

Um, and if it's not then it's not being pulled in.

Our actual listings on a couple of these.

I think that's great because if, if there is a mistake, it's gonna help us understand.

For example, if, if there's an error on the apartments.com side, we can work with them to make sure that they're accurate or if it's on our side and we need to update some forms or our own website, um, knowing where you got that incorrect information is going to be helpful for everybody.

Mhm.

And we know for a fact some of them were in between or it wasn't, that wasn't the case.

Now,
that's why we're here, things that jump out of us, we can only compare to what we know.

Um Andrew did it just to see how does apartment I Q's comp set compare to the information that was gathered, you know, the old school way of just going out and gathering the data yourself.
And so those are the types of things Andrew's mentioning here as, as things that we knew to be incorrect in that situation.
Now, if we can figure out how to make this 100% or at least close to it, it's so much faster and has so much more detail than what we were able to get before.
So the use case is still really great um from acquisitions through asset management.
So just gotta work through some of the tweaks.
Yes, I'll echo.

That was saying, um, we're currently listing a deal in Shreveport, Louisiana as a deal that we're trying to sell and the broker that we use in the area didn't necessarily choose the best comps in my opinion.
So my first type was apartment IQ and kind of is like, hey, what about these different deals that kind of built in as our competitors?
And they ended up, I think I gave them three suggestions and they included them all in the next term.
So that was super helpful and really good use of the software.
So I appreciate the help on that
in general.

Have you found the, the comp uh the way that we're pulling comps to be factoring in the things that you would like to see, is there anything else that you'd want to see the, the algorithm weighed more heavily towards?
And
I can't think of it off the top of my head,
at least from my perspective.
What we need is, is there um you know, talk to us a year from now and, and I'm sure we'll have all sorts of great ideas, but
we spend so much time trying to understand if we have a classic unit and we renovate it.
Where will we sit compared to the comps after it's renovated?
And so being able to see that and customize our comp set around what's renovated and what isn't?
That's a huge win right there.

Um, because that's one of the reasons some of the other third party sources are so difficult to work with because you can't see what properties have renovated and what's what their classic rents are versus an upgraded unit.

So that piece and then the fees, um, you know, there's litigation, we're getting wind of potential litigation in the future around unnecessary fees just like they did with some of the other industries.
And we wanna understand what are we charging versus the comps so that we can make sure that what the resident is looking to pay within their budget is appropriate on our websites.
Right.
Well, those sound like strong use cases.
Sounds like it's the, you, you are seeing some of the efficiency gains of the automation in the system.
So that's great.
And um, we, we're more than committed to working together to keep making sure that system gets better and better for you.
All right.
I have a broader question.
Um, kind of, you know, assuming rents and occupancy are probably, you know, two of the top data points that we are curious about in any given, you know, use case.
Um, you know, rents, rents, I think are fairly simple.
I, I understand, you know, you scrape the data from the website.
You know, there's the rent on the website, you know, you put it into your system.
Um, obviously the occupancy, um you know, is kind of a bigger question around publicly available data versus not publicly available data.

Um So, you know, as you guys offer kind of this advertised occupancy percentage, um you know, I wonder just kind of generally speaking, you know, how you guys think about it um in terms of, you know, is this um better than, you know, previously available sources of occupancy data, you know, and, and, and why, and then I kind of note, um I noticed that uh when you hover over the advertised occupancy, the definition pops up, um and it says accuracy is dependent on property advertising practices.
Just kind of curious, you know, what, what that means.
And if there's any, you know, circumstances that you guys have run into that um would maybe make this data less reliable.
And if there's any way that, you know, we can understand when that might be happening or, or if it's um more difficult to understand.
Yeah, absolutely.
Um The short answer is we're working on uh function a confidence score right now for metrics like occupancy.
So, uh it's not, it's, it's quite straightforward for us to detect if a property is actually able to hide availability.
Uh It's almost always in properties that are maybe performing a bit lower than they'd like them to be or um sometimes on li ups uh in the way that they attack us.
So our system can see if a property is cycling units.
They can see if uh they're saying that they're 99% occupied, but they have a two month concession, that's gonna be a red flag.
So there will be a functional confidence for or, or uh something very similar to that, that we're working on right now that I would anticipate you'd see in the coming weeks.

That would give you an indication of, hey, this is a property where those advertising practices are creating a scenario where with only publicly available data, it's gonna be, you know, you should, you should take this occupancy number with a grain of salt.
Um
What I would say beyond that is uh those cases are not that common.
Um uh The, the primary way that folks think about hiding availability is they will limit the number of units they show per floor plan.
Um You know, capping their floor plans functionally.
Uh There are if, if you, so what our system does is it will go to a website and it will change the move in date, it will change filters, et cetera.
And so if you query the website in enough different ways, you're typically able to unearth those units that may uh um on just first glance, you might not be able to see for a given floor plan.
Um So often we're actually able to detect units that you that, that you might not see on first glance um that are truly actually available behind that.
Um I I would say in terms of like overall accuracy.
And can you do this from public data only?
I think the vast majority of the time that we've heard is yes.
So we Q A this with um Willow Bridge's Revenue Management team the other day and they found that our occupancy for stabilized properties was within 0.5% of their actual properties.
So that system, it, it is functionally the the way the system works is, it's of course, looking at properties, there are units that are on the market now that we know are vacant and unleashed.
But the system is also factoring in vacant leased units.

Um We can get some sense of if we think there's some down units of the property because we've seen the property lease over a number of years, we could start to see uh if there's units that that should be coming back on the market but or not.
And so there's an algorithm in the background, basically predicting the likelihood that a given unit on the website now and that has been released recently is vacant or not.
That algorithm is obviously getting better and better over time.
And we wanna make sure we're transparent with where we think it's strongest and where we think it's not as strong yet.

But we do overall think you can get a fairly accurate occupancy number, especially when you consider um you know, the big comparison point we think about is in the past occupancy was gathered manually and intermittently and when you look at how many leases are happening and what's happening at these properties, even just cancels denials versus applications that are happening every single day.

We think the frequency of the updates uh in, in general outweighs um which any, any of those weaknesses that at least historically, that's what we've heard from our customers and that's sort of our North Star in terms of how we think about it.
Yeah.
Yeah.

No, that's super helpful and, and obviously, yeah, as you kind of mentioned, you know, there's weaknesses of previous methods of, of gathering, whether it's, you know, call around which, you know, anyone can say anything or um you know, other sources that may be outdated, um you know, a month behind whatever the case may be.

Um So that makes sense and, and yeah, it um you would think that it would benefit an apartment owner or manager to, to be showing all of the units that are available to cast the wide net and, and you know, to have all their units in case someone wants, you know, that specific unit or that price or feature or whatever it is.
Um But, you know, I can also see where there are situations where they, you know, might not want to um have that data out there.

So, um so that, that's very helpful and, and yeah, I'd be very interested to see some sort of confidence score in the future just you know, because that data point seems to be a little bit more um you know, uh hard to, hard to see through, I guess.
Um So yeah, thanks for that answer.
Appreciate it.
I was I was gonna bring up the occupancy.
Sorry, I like to interrupt and the available units, but it looks like we are, we're culprits of hiding units.

So it's accurate on your end and I'm not sure why we're hiding four units on our end.
We certainly shouldn't be.

The first unit trends is what ALEC was mentioning is we're capturing these individual units every single day and over up to four years now.

What's that strategy been?
Um And obviously, then all your high level KPIS your your size of the floor plan days on market availability, et cetera.
So just a lot more transparency and drill down is just even more uh unit specific.

When there was a concession introduced, how quickly did the unit lease?
And then of course, I know a big use case here is how quickly renovated units um are leased.

You'll start to see unit specific amenities too.

Um And so often you'll start to get interesting.
Sometimes you'll see floor plan level like this uh property, you're getting a sense of uh floor plan level across these units, but often you'll see differences in views here.
Uh sometimes proximity to amenities, parking, accessibility, et cetera.

And those can help explain and obviously the floor they're on as well that can help explain differences in prices as you're really trying to understand apples to apples comparisons uh versus the comp.
